FW-01.origin: The MX L3 firewall ends with an implicit allow-any-any outbound rule, so anything not explicitly denied egresses freely. A default-deny (allowlist) outbound posture is a long-standing least-privilege principle codified in CIS Controls and NIST SP 800-41. There is no single 'first incident' — the practice grew from decades of egress-filtering guidance aimed at containing malware command-and-control and data exfiltration.
pros: Blocks malware C2 and data exfiltration over unexpected ports/destinations
Enforces least-privilege egress; only sanctioned traffic leaves
Maps to CIS/NIST compliance
Surfaces shadow IT and unknown outbound flows
cons: Requires enumerating every legitimate outbound flow first or you break apps
Higher ongoing maintenance as new services are added
Risk of outages if the allow rules above the deny are incomplete
FW-01.ifNotSet: Any compromised host or rogue app on the LAN can reach arbitrary internet destinations and ports, enabling C2 callbacks and silent data exfiltration.
FW-01.ifSet: Only explicitly permitted destinations/ports egress; everything else is dropped, shrinking the exfiltration surface at the cost of maintaining an accurate allowlist.

FW-02.origin: A custom L3 rule with source Any, destination Any, protocol Any set to Allow is functionally a no-op that defeats the surrounding ruleset and is indistinguishable from having no policy. The anti-pattern is called out across firewall hardening standards (NIST SP 800-41, CIS) as an overly permissive rule. It derives from the least-privilege/deny-by-default model that has governed packet filters since the early 1990s.
pros: Removes a rule that silently nullifies tighter rules around it
Forces explicit, auditable allow rules scoped to real needs
Reduces lateral movement and unintended inter-VLAN reachability
cons: Tightening may break traffic relying on the broad allow
Requires discovering exactly what the any/any rule was permitting
FW-02.ifNotSet: An any/any allow rule overrides more specific controls below it (top-down first-match), effectively leaving that traffic path unfiltered.
FW-02.ifSet: Traffic must match a specific, scoped allow rule to pass, restoring least-privilege; some previously-permitted flows may need explicit rules added.

FW-03.origin: Meraki's IP Source Address Spoofing Protection implements unicast Reverse Path Forwarding (uRPF), validating that a packet's source IP/VLAN is consistent before forwarding. The underlying defense is BCP 38 / RFC 2827 (Network Ingress Filtering, Ferguson & Senie, 2000), written to curb spoofed-source DoS and reflection attacks. Networks created on/after 2018-07-12 default to 'Block'; older ones default to 'Log', which only alerts.
pros: Drops packets with forged/inconsistent source IPs (uRPF / BCP 38)
Mitigates spoofing-based DoS, reflection, and host impersonation
Default-on for modern networks; Meraki best practice
cons: Asymmetric routing or unusual topologies can cause legitimate drops
Requires correct subnet/static-route definitions to avoid false positives
FW-03.ifNotSet: In 'Log' (or disabled) mode the MX forwards spoofed or VLAN-inconsistent packets, letting attackers impersonate hosts and bypass source-IP controls while only logging.
FW-03.ifSet: Traffic failing source/VLAN validation is dropped at the appliance; correct subnet and static-route config is needed to avoid dropping valid asymmetric flows.

refs.title: IP Source Address Spoofing Protection — Meraki

EXP-01.origin: Each MX port-forwarding rule has an 'Allowed remote IPs' field; leaving it at 'Any' exposes the forwarded internal service to the entire internet. Meraki's General MX Best Practices explicitly advise narrowing this scope. This reflects the classic attack-surface-reduction principle — the smaller the set of permitted sources, the smaller the population that can reach (and attack) the service.
pros: Limits who can reach the forwarded service to known source IPs
Cuts exposure to internet-wide scanning and brute-force bots
Follows Meraki's documented best practice
cons: Source IP allowlists break for clients on dynamic/unknown addresses
Adds maintenance as legitimate remote IPs change
Not feasible for services that must be truly public
EXP-01.ifNotSet: A port-forward open to any remote IP is reachable by every host on the internet, continuously scanned, exposing any backend vulnerability or weak credential to the world.
EXP-01.ifSet: Only the listed remote IP(s) can connect to the forwarded port, removing the service from internet-wide reachability at the cost of maintaining the allowlist.

refs.title: Port Forwarding and NAT Rules on the MX — Meraki
Blocking Inbound Traffic on MX — Meraki

EXP-02.origin: Exposing management/file-sharing ports (RDP 3389, SSH 22, SMB 445, Telnet 23, DB ports) directly to the internet is one of the most exploited misconfigurations. SMB 445 exposure was the vector for the 2017 WannaCry and NotPetya worms (via EternalBlue, MS17-010), and internet-facing RDP 3389 is a perennial top entry point for ransomware brute-force campaigns documented by CISA.
pros: Removes high-value, heavily-targeted services from internet exposure
Defends against RDP/SSH brute-force and SMB worm propagation
Pushes remote access onto VPN/zero-trust paths
cons: Admins lose direct external access and must use VPN/jump host
May require re-architecting existing remote-access workflows
EXP-02.ifNotSet: An internet-exposed sensitive port (RDP/SSH/SMB) is a prime target for automated brute-force and exploit traffic and a leading initial-access vector for ransomware and worms like WannaCry.
EXP-02.ifSet: The sensitive service is no longer directly reachable from the internet; remote access shifts to VPN or a restricted remote-IP allowlist, dramatically cutting exposure.

refs.title: Port Forwarding and NAT Rules on the MX — Meraki

EXP-03.origin: 1:1 NAT maps a dedicated public IP to a single internal host, giving that server a persistent, directly-addressable internet presence. Not a vulnerability by itself, but an exposure-surface signal worth auditing — Meraki best practice is to scope each mapping with explicit allowed remote IPs and port ranges. A permanent public mapping to an internal host should be intentional and tightly bounded.
pros: Forces review of every internal host given a public IP
Encourages scoping mappings to specific ports and remote IPs
Identifies dormant or forgotten public exposure for removal
cons: Legitimate hosted services (mail/web) may genuinely require 1:1 NAT
Removing a mapping breaks any inbound dependency on that public IP
EXP-03.ifNotSet: Each 1:1 NAT mapping presents an internal host on a dedicated public IP; if its allowed remote IPs/ports are broad, that host's services are reachable and attackable from the internet.
EXP-03.ifSet: Mappings are inventoried, each justified and scoped (specific ports + allowed remote IPs), or removed if unneeded, shrinking the externally reachable footprint.

refs.title: Port Forwarding and NAT Rules on the MX — Meraki

EXP-04.origin: On the MX, the outbound L3 firewall does NOT filter inbound traffic arriving via port-forwarding or 1:1 NAT — that traffic is governed only by each rule's own 'Allowed remote IPs' field, not by the L3 ruleset. This is the most common Meraki misconception: engineers assume a tight L3 policy also constrains NAT'd inbound flows. It does not. Documented in Meraki's 'Blocking Inbound Traffic on MX' guide.
pros: Closes the gap where NAT'd inbound traffic is otherwise unfiltered by L3
Forces per-rule allowed-remote-IP scoping where it actually applies
Corrects a widespread false sense of security about the L3 policy
cons: Must configure restrictions on each forwarding/NAT rule individually
Misunderstanding can lead to over-blocking or continued exposure
EXP-04.ifNotSet: Because NAT'd inbound traffic bypasses the L3 firewall, a network that looks locked down by its L3 rules can still be wide open on every NAT'd port to any remote IP.
EXP-04.ifSet: Inbound exposure is controlled where it actually takes effect — the per-rule 'Allowed remote IPs' field — so NAT'd services are genuinely restricted rather than only appearing to be.

refs.title: Blocking Inbound Traffic on MX — Meraki

EXP-05.origin: An inbound firewall rule (used with no-NAT or VPN-concentrator deployments) that is overly broad over-permits externally-initiated traffic. The flag derives from the same least-privilege and attack-surface-reduction principles in NIST SP 800-41 and CIS guidance: inbound allows should be the minimum specific tuples required.
pros: Narrows externally-initiated reachability to only required sources/ports
Reduces exposed attack surface for inbound-permitted services
Aligns inbound policy with least-privilege / NIST 800-41
cons: Tightening can break inbound flows relying on the broad rule
Requires accurate inventory of legitimate inbound sources and ports
EXP-05.ifNotSet: A broad inbound allow rule lets a wide range of external sources reach internal hosts/ports, expanding the attack surface well beyond what any service requires.
EXP-05.ifSet: Inbound access is constrained to specific sources, destinations, and ports; some previously-working flows may need explicit narrower rules.

refs.title: Blocking Inbound Traffic on MX — Meraki

THR-01.origin: The MX intrusion detection/prevention engine is built on Snort, the open-source IDS/IPS first released by Martin Roesch in 1998, with rules curated by Cisco Talos. In 'Detection' mode the MX only logs malicious traffic; only 'Prevention' mode places the engine inline to actively drop matched exploit traffic. The threat model is network-borne exploitation of known CVEs; rulesets are scoped by severity.
pros: Actively blocks known exploits inline rather than only logging
Talos-curated rules auto-update from the cloud
Severity-tunable rulesets limit false positives
cons: Inline blocking can drop legitimate traffic matching a noisy signature
Adds inspection latency/throughput cost on the MX
Requires Advanced Security or SD-WAN licensing
THR-01.ifNotSet: In Detection mode (or off) the MX sees malicious traffic but lets it through, so a known-exploit attempt can succeed while only generating an alert.
THR-01.ifSet: In Prevention mode the MX drops matched exploit traffic at the edge in real time; tune to Balanced/Connectivity if a signature causes false-positive blocks.

THR-02.origin: AMP (Advanced Malware Protection) is the anti-malware engine from Sourcefire, which Cisco acquired in 2013 (same lineage as Snort/Talos). On the MX it inspects HTTP file downloads and queries the AMP cloud for a disposition (clean/malicious/unknown), blocking malicious files and issuing retrospective alerts when a previously-allowed file is reclassified. It reflects the shift to cloud reputation after the 2000s explosion of polymorphic malware that signature-only AV missed.
pros: Cloud file-reputation blocks known-malicious downloads before they land
Retrospective detection re-flags files that turn malicious after delivery
Zero local signature maintenance
cons: Only inspects unencrypted HTTP downloads, so HTTPS-delivered malware can be missed
Requires Advanced Security licensing and Traffic analysis enabled
THR-02.ifNotSet: With AMP disabled, downloads pass with no reputation check, so a host can pull a known-malicious payload the AMP cloud would otherwise have blocked.
THR-02.ifSet: Malicious-disposition downloads are blocked and retrospective alerts fire for files later reclassified; ensure Traffic Analysis is on for it to function.

THR-03.origin: MX content filtering uses Cisco Talos URL categorization to classify destinations by content/threat category, evaluated against the URL in HTTP payloads or the SNI of TLS traffic. Beyond acceptable-use, blocking high-risk categories (malware sites, phishing, newly-registered domains, C2) cuts off malware delivery and exfiltration channels. Reflected in CIS Controls (malware defenses, web/DNS filtering) and NIST boundary-protection guidance.
pros: Blocks known-bad categories (malware, phishing, C2) as a delivery-channel control
Talos categorization auto-classifies sites with no manual upkeep
Custom block/allow URL patterns layer on top, allow-list wins
cons: Category miscategorization can over-block legitimate business sites
SNI/HTTP inspection can be evaded by encrypted DNS/ESNI
THR-03.ifNotSet: With no categories or patterns configured, all web destinations are reachable, leaving an open path to malware-hosting, phishing, and C2 sites.
THR-03.ifSet: Requests to blocked categories/patterns are denied (allow-list takes precedence); validate against Talos category lookups to avoid over-blocking.

WIFI-02.origin: WPA1 (original Wi-Fi Protected Access, 2003) relies on TKIP/RC4, a stopgap that wrapped the broken WEP cipher. Beck and Tews demonstrated a practical TKIP attack in 2008. The 2017 KRACK key-reinstallation attack by Mathy Vanhoef showed that with TKIP the impact is 'especially catastrophic' — decryption plus forgery and injection — versus AES-CCMP. On a Meraki MR, allowing 'WPA1 and WPA2' enables mixed mode that still permits TKIP.
pros: WPA2-only forces AES-CCMP, eliminating weak TKIP/RC4
Removes the severe KRACK forgery/injection exposure tied to TKIP
Nearly all modern clients support WPA2/WPA3
cons: Disconnects genuinely legacy clients that only support WPA1/TKIP
May require firmware/driver updates on old IoT/industrial gear
WIFI-02.ifNotSet: An SSID that still allows WPA1/TKIP exposes traffic to known TKIP weaknesses and the worst-case KRACK injection/forgery scenario.
WIFI-02.ifSet: Switching to WPA2-only (AES) or WPA3 closes the TKIP weakness; the tradeoff is that any client incapable of WPA2 can no longer associate.

refs.title: Setting a WPA Encryption Mode — Meraki

WIFI-05.origin: A default or brand SSID leaks hardware-fingerprint information to anyone in radio range, aiding reconnaissance and targeting of model-specific vulnerabilities and default credentials. Default SSIDs also appear in precomputed PSK/rainbow dictionaries because the SSID is used as the salt in WPA-PSK key derivation (IEEE 802.11i), making offline cracking of weak passphrases faster.
pros: Removes a hardware/vendor fingerprint that aids reconnaissance
Breaks reuse of precomputed SSID-salted PSK dictionaries
Forces deliberate naming over a factory default
cons: Renaming an in-use SSID forces all clients to reconnect
Cosmetic on its own — must pair with strong WPA2/WPA3 + strong PSK
WIFI-05.ifNotSet: A default/brand SSID advertises the gear in use to passive observers and may match canned cracking dictionaries, lowering the effort to fingerprint and attack.
WIFI-05.ifSet: A unique, non-revealing SSID reduces the reconnaissance footprint and defeats SSID-keyed PSK dictionaries; expect a one-time client reconnect.

WIFI-07.origin: Air Marshal is Meraki's built-in WIPS. The 'seen on LAN' classification is the serious one: the AP inspects traffic on its own wired port and correlates broadcast BSSIDs against MACs observed on the wired LAN, flagging an SSID whose radio is also bridged onto your wired network — a strong indicator of an unsanctioned AP plugged into the corporate LAN. The threat model is the classic rogue access point. PCI-DSS mandates rogue-AP detection.
pros: Correlates wireless BSSIDs to wired-LAN MACs to catch bridged APs
Built into the MR with no separate sensor, plus alerting
Supports containment of confirmed rogues via deauthentication
cons: Containment via deauth has legal/regulatory implications and can hit neighbors
BSSID/MAC heuristics can produce false positives
A true 'seen on LAN' hit requires physical investigation to locate
WIFI-07.ifNotSet: An unaddressed rogue/wired-seen AP may be bridging your trusted wired LAN to an open or attacker-controlled wireless segment, bypassing perimeter and NAC controls.
WIFI-07.ifSet: Classifying and containing the rogue (and physically removing a 'seen on LAN' AP) closes the bridge; use containment carefully since deauth can disrupt nearby networks.

ANOM-01.origin: A 169.254.x.x address is an IPv4 link-local address defined by RFC 3927 (also called APIPA). A host self-assigns one only when it cannot obtain configuration from a DHCP server, so the address is a direct symptom that DHCP failed. Common Meraki root causes: an exhausted DHCP pool, an unreachable DHCP server (downtime, broken VPN, or firewall-rule change), or a VLAN-tag mismatch between the SSID and the upstream trunk's native VLAN.
pros: An unambiguous, easy-to-detect signal that DHCP failed for that client
Points straight at pool exhaustion, server reachability, or VLAN-tag faults
Catches scope/lease problems before they cascade across many clients
cons: The address is a symptom, not the cause — root-cause still needs investigation
Transient cases (brief blip, then recovery) can produce noise
ANOM-01.ifNotSet: A client stuck on 169.254.x.x has no routable IP or gateway and cannot reach the network; left unaddressed it signals a DHCP issue that will keep stranding more clients.
ANOM-01.ifSet: Once DHCP is fixed (free pool addresses, restore server reachability, or correct the VLAN tag) the client renews into a routable lease; confirm pool capacity and no rogue DHCP server competes.

ANOM-02.origin: A duplicate-IP (IP conflict) is when two devices use the same IPv4 address on one L2 segment. The MX detects two different MACs sending traffic with the same source IP and logs a conflict; detection relies on ARP. Conflicts usually stem from a rogue DHCP server handing out overlapping leases or a static IP set inside an active DHCP scope. Both devices suffer intermittent or broken connectivity until resolved.
pros: Catches a real connectivity-breaking fault — both devices intermittently drop
Often exposes a rogue DHCP server or a static IP inside the DHCP scope
Conflict events log both MACs, making offenders traceable to a port
cons: Some conflicts are transient/benign (e.g. an iOS device reusing a prior IP)
Finding the second device still requires correlating MAC-to-port
ANOM-02.ifNotSet: While two devices share an IP, both experience dropped/intermittent traffic and ARP thrash; the underlying rogue DHCP server or mis-set static keeps generating conflicts.
ANOM-02.ifSet: Resolving the conflict (remove the static from the DHCP range, or hunt the rogue DHCP server) restores stable addressing; verify no further conflict events recur.

OVR-IDS.origin: Meraki configuration templates bind one base config to many site networks so fleet-wide hardening trickles down. The docs are explicit that any local change on a bound network overrides the template's value. When a site's IDS/IPS is set weaker than the template (Prevention dropped to Detection or off), the template's protection silently no longer applies on that site — classic config drift.
pros: Re-aligning restores Prevention-mode IDS/IPS fleet-wide with no per-site work
Eliminates a silent gap where a site looks compliant but isn't
Centralized rulesets stay consistent and auto-update
cons: Prevention (inline IPS) can add latency or false-positive drops
A legitimate site-specific exception may have been the reason for the override
OVR-IDS.ifNotSet: This network runs weaker-than-template intrusion protection while the org dashboard implies it is hardened, so traffic the template would block can pass undetected here.
OVR-IDS.ifSet: Restoring the template value (or re-binding) returns this network to parity with the fleet; re-validate any traffic the site genuinely needed to allow.

OVR-AMP.origin: AMP inspects HTTP file downloads on the MX and blocks malicious dispositions; normally pushed by the bound template. Per Meraki's template docs, a local change overrides the template, so disabling AMP on this site silently undoes the template's malware defense for it. AMP also depends on Traffic analysis being enabled, so an override there can disable it indirectly.
pros: Re-aligning restores retrospective HTTP malware blocking on this site
Keeps disposition/allow-list policy consistent with the fleet
Closes a silent gap the org-wide view would hide
cons: AMP requires Advanced Security licensing and Traffic analysis enabled
Only inspects HTTP file downloads (not HTTPS), so not total coverage
OVR-AMP.ifNotSet: Malicious file downloads the template would block can complete on this network, and retrospective alerts will not fire for this site.
OVR-AMP.ifSet: Re-enabling AMP to match the template restores download inspection; verify Advanced Security licensing and that Traffic analysis is on, or AMP will not run.

OVR-SPOOF.origin: IP Source Address Spoofing Protection uses uRPF-style VLAN validation to drop traffic whose source IP doesn't match its VLAN. It runs in Block or Log mode; networks after 2018-07-12 default to Block. When bound to a template, a local override dropping this site to Log (or off) silently weakens posture per Meraki's rule that local changes override the template.
pros: Block mode actively drops spoofed/impersonation traffic
Re-aligning gives uniform anti-spoofing posture across sites
Aligns with BCP 38 source-address validation
cons: Strict VLAN validation can drop traffic in asymmetric-routing topologies
Disabled in passthrough/VPN-concentrator mode
OVR-SPOOF.ifNotSet: In Log-only/disabled state this network records spoofing but lets packets through, so an attacker can impersonate a host while the fleet template implies Block is enforced.
OVR-SPOOF.ifSet: Restoring Block to match the template drops spoofed traffic; validate intentional asymmetric/cross-VLAN flows first.

OVR-L3.origin: MX L3 outbound rules are stateful, matched top-down with an implicit allow-all at the end. Templates push a standardized ruleset to every bound site. Because a local change on a bound network overrides the template, a site whose L3 rules were edited locally no longer enforces the template's intended segmentation/egress policy — often more permissively given the trailing implicit allow.
pros: Re-aligning restores the template's vetted egress/segmentation rules
Removes accidental permissive gaps from local edits
Keeps rule ordering and intent consistent across the fleet
cons: A local rule may exist for a genuine site-specific need
Mis-ordered restored rules can block required traffic (first-match wins)
OVR-L3.ifNotSet: This network enforces a different (often weaker) outbound policy than the template intends, so traffic the template would block may be allowed here while the org view implies otherwise.
OVR-L3.ifSet: Restoring the template's L3 rules re-establishes intended egress control; re-verify any legitimate site-specific flow still has an explicit permit before the catch-all.

SW-NV1.origin: VLAN 1 is the default native (untagged) VLAN on every Meraki trunk and the default access VLAN for unconfigured ports, so it spans the L2 domain. The double-tagging VLAN-hopping attack documented in Steve Rouiller's @stake/SANS 'Virtual LAN Security: Weaknesses and Countermeasures' (2003) relies on the attacker's access VLAN matching a trunk's native VLAN: a frame tagged inner+outer (outer = native) has its outer tag stripped by the first switch and is forwarded into the victim VLAN. A dedicated, unused native VLAN removes the precondition.
pros: Removes the precondition for double-tagging VLAN-hopping attacks
Aligns with CIS and Cisco L2 hardening guidance
Native VLAN no longer carries user/data traffic on the trunk
cons: Native VLAN must match on both trunk ends or you bridge traffic / mismatch
On switch-to-switch trunks the native VLAN still carries untagged control traffic — change carefully
SW-NV1.ifNotSet: An attacker on an access port in VLAN 1 can craft double-tagged frames that hop into other VLANs across the trunk, defeating the L2 segmentation the VLANs enforce.
SW-NV1.ifSet: Trunks carry an unused, dedicated native VLAN, neutralizing double-tagging; ensure both ends of every trunk agree on the new native VLAN to avoid a mismatch.

refs.title: Virtual LAN Security: Weaknesses and Countermeasures (SANS/Rouiller)

SW-TRUNK.origin: A trunk set to allow 'all' VLANs (1-4094) extends every broadcast domain to that link, widening the blast radius for storms and exposing every VLAN to whatever sits on the other end. Meraki's General MS Best Practices explicitly advises pruning unnecessary VLANs off trunks to reduce flooding scope. Combined with switch-spoofing or rogue-switch attacks, an unpruned trunk hands an attacker reach into VLANs that should never be exposed there.
pros: Limits flood/broadcast scope to only the VLANs a link needs
Reduces lateral L2 reachability if the far end is compromised
Matches Meraki best-practice VLAN pruning
cons: Over-pruning breaks legitimate traffic — the list must include every needed VLAN
Allowed lists must be kept in sync on both ends and updated as VLANs change
SW-TRUNK.ifNotSet: Every VLAN can traverse the trunk, so a single compromised device behind it gains reach into all segments and broadcast storms propagate everywhere.
SW-TRUNK.ifSet: The trunk only carries explicitly listed VLANs, containing floods and limiting lateral movement; you maintain accurate allowed-VLAN lists as topology changes.

SW-UNUSED.origin: An enabled-but-disconnected access port is an open L2 jack: anyone with physical access can plug in and land directly on the access VLAN with no authentication. This is the classic 'unused-port' hardening item in the CIS Cisco benchmarks and Cisco campus guidance — shut down unused ports or park them in a black-hole VLAN. It is the physical-access counterpart to 802.1X: if the port can't come up, no L2 attacks can be launched from it.
pros: Closes open physical jacks that would grant unauthenticated L2 access
Removes a launch point for rogue-DHCP, STP, MAC-flood and hopping attacks
Standard CIS / Cisco hardening control, cheap to apply
cons: A port merely temporarily link-down (idle workstation, seasonal device) may actually be in use — disabling causes an outage when it returns
SW-UNUSED.ifNotSet: Every live, unused jack is an unauthenticated entry point onto the access VLAN; an intruder with physical access can connect and begin L2 attacks.
SW-UNUSED.ifSet: The port is administratively down and rejects any device plugged in; a legitimate future device will need the port re-enabled (ideally with an access policy) first.

SW-PORTSEC.origin: An access port with no access policy authenticates nothing — any device that links up is on the network. 802.1X (IEEE 802.1X port-based access control) and MAC Authentication Bypass require devices to prove identity to a RADIUS server before the port forwards user traffic. The control counters MAC flooding (the classic dsniff 'macof' CAM-overflow attack) and unauthorized-device attachment.
pros: Forces device/user authentication before any L2 access
Counters unauthorized attachment and MAC-flooding (CAM overflow)
Supports phased rollout via Open mode before Closed
cons: Requires a reachable RADIUS server — outage/misconfig can lock out users
Headless/legacy devices may need MAB exceptions, weakening the control
Closed mode applied too aggressively can disrupt connectivity
SW-PORTSEC.ifNotSet: Any device plugged into an access port joins with no authentication, so a rogue laptop or MAC-flooding tool can attach and operate freely on the access VLAN.
SW-PORTSEC.ifSet: Ports require 802.1X/MAB authentication before forwarding, blocking unauthorized devices; you must run a RADIUS server and handle exceptions for devices that can't do 802.1X.

SW-DHCP.origin: If the switch accepts DHCP offers from any device, an attacker can stand up a rogue DHCP server to hand out a malicious gateway/DNS and man-in-the-middle traffic, or run DHCP starvation (Yersinia, Gobbler) to exhaust the pool and take over addressing. Meraki's DHCP Guard filters DHCP/RA messages by policy — Allow with a block-list or Deny with an allow-list of trusted server MACs. It is the Meraki analogue of Cisco DHCP Snooping.
pros: Blocks rogue/unauthorized DHCP servers from issuing addresses (anti-MITM)
Mitigates DHCP starvation by restricting which servers may respond
Detected servers are surfaced in the dashboard for quick allow/block
cons: Default-Deny with an allow-list breaks DHCP if a legitimate server's MAC is omitted
Maintenance burden as DHCP servers are added/replaced/fail over
SW-DHCP.ifNotSet: A rogue DHCP server can hand clients a malicious gateway/DNS and silently MITM their traffic, or starve the real pool so no client can get an address.
SW-DHCP.ifSet: Only trusted DHCP servers may respond, neutralizing rogue-server MITM and starvation; keep the allow-list accurate so legitimate (including failover) servers aren't blocked.

refs.title: Tracking Down Rogue DHCP Servers — Meraki

SW-STP.origin: RSTP (IEEE 802.1w) prevents Layer-2 loops and the broadcast storms / MAC-table instability they cause. With RSTP disabled, a single accidental loop can melt the L2 domain, and there is no defense against STP root-bridge takeover — Yersinia can inject superior BPDUs to force itself to become root and intercept traffic. Meraki enables RSTP globally by default and recommends pairing it with BPDU Guard / Root Guard.
pros: Prevents Layer-2 loops and the storms/MAC instability they cause
Foundation for STP guards (BPDU Guard, Root Guard, Loop Guard)
Enabled by default and recommended by Meraki
cons: Rarely, RSTP can interoperate poorly with a misbehaving third-party device (disable per-port instead, not globally)
SW-STP.ifNotSet: Any L2 loop — accidental cabling or malicious injection — can trigger a broadcast storm that brings down the network, with no protection against STP root-bridge hijacking.
SW-STP.ifSet: RSTP actively blocks loops and, with BPDU/Root Guard, resists root-bridge attacks; keep guards configured on edge and uplink ports.

refs.title: Configuring Spanning Tree on MS — Meraki

SW-STORM.origin: Storm control rate-limits broadcast, multicast, and unknown-unicast floods so a single misbehaving host, loop, or flooding attack can't saturate a link. The threat model is the broadcast/multicast storm — from a cabling loop, faulty NIC, or deliberate flooding (an L2 DoS related to macof/Yersinia tooling). Meraki's Enhanced Storm Control suppresses those classes by a configurable percentage (1-99%) of interface bandwidth.
pros: Caps broadcast/multicast/unknown-unicast floods before they saturate a link
Limits blast radius of loops, faulty NICs, and flooding-based L2 DoS
Per-port granularity once enabled globally
cons: Thresholds set too low can drop legitimate bursty broadcast/multicast (imaging, video, discovery)
Only on supported MS / Catalyst models, so coverage may be uneven
SW-STORM.ifNotSet: A broadcast/multicast/unknown-unicast storm can consume all link bandwidth and take the segment offline, with only basic control-plane policing as a backstop.
SW-STORM.ifSet: Flood traffic is held under the configured percentage so storms are contained; tune thresholds so legitimate high-volume broadcast/multicast apps aren't throttled.

OPS-01.origin: Patch-currency is foundational hygiene: outdated firmware retains known, already-patched CVEs that automated scanners exploit first. The discipline traces to CIS Control 7 (Continuous Vulnerability Management) and NIST SP 800-40, not a single Meraki incident. Meraki publishes 'stable/Recommended' trains so operators stay current without riding bleeding-edge betas.
pros: Closes known, already-patched vulnerabilities before exploitation
Picks up stability/bug fixes Cisco has validated on the stable train
Keeps you eligible for full TAC support
Unlocks newer dashboard/security features tied to firmware
cons: Upgrades require a maintenance window and brief reboots/downtime
A new stable build can introduce regressions; rollback may be needed
Scheduling across many networks takes coordination
OPS-01.ifNotSet: Devices keep running code with publicly documented vulnerabilities and unpatched bugs, widening the attack surface and risking instability.
OPS-01.ifSet: You gain the security/stability of the current stable train, at the cost of planning windows and validating each release for regressions before broad rollout.

OPS-02.origin: Centralized log export is a core requirement of nearly every incident-response and audit standard — NIST SP 800-92 (log management), CIS Control 8 (Audit Log Management), PCI DSS Req. 10. Meraki devices keep only a short dashboard event window, so without syslog (and SNMP for state polling) there is no durable, queryable record for forensics or alerting. The rule exists because 'we couldn't tell what happened' is the recurring post-incident failure.
pros: Durable off-box log retention for forensics and audit
Feeds a SIEM for correlation, alerting, and compliance (PCI Req.10)
SNMP enables proactive state/health polling
Tamper-evident record an on-device attacker can't easily erase
cons: Requires standing up and securing a syslog/SIEM and SNMP collector
Log volume and storage/retention costs must be managed
SNMPv2c community strings are weak — v3 (SHA/AES) is needed
OPS-02.ifNotSet: When an incident occurs you have little to no historical record to investigate, and no external system watches device state — intrusions can go unnoticed and unprovable, failing audit/compliance.
OPS-02.ifSet: You get durable logs and proactive monitoring, but take on running collectors, securing transport (TLS syslog / SNMPv3), and managing storage.

OPS-03.origin: Dashboard alerts are Meraki's built-in detection layer: device-down, configuration-change, VPN-down, rogue AP, DHCP/IP-conflict, usage-threshold. With every alert disabled, the operator has no automated signal that something broke or changed — violating the 'detect and respond promptly' intent behind NIST CSF (Detect) and CIS Control 8/17. The configuration-change alert is a lightweight tamper/insider tripwire.
pros: Real-time email/webhook/push notice of outages, config changes, security events
Config-change alerts act as a tripwire for unauthorized changes
Cheap to enable — native to the dashboard
Can route to Slack/Teams/PagerDuty via webhooks
cons: Poorly tuned thresholds cause alert fatigue
Email-only delivery can be missed without webhook/escalation
OPS-03.ifNotSet: Outages, device failures, VPN drops, and unauthorized config changes happen silently — the first you hear is a user complaint or a breach.
OPS-03.ifSet: You get timely visibility into failures and changes, but must tune which alerts fire and to whom so the signal stays actionable.

VPN-01.origin: Site-to-site (Auto) VPN is the encrypted backbone carrying inter-site traffic; if a tunnel is down or a peer unreachable, branch traffic may fail or silently fall back to a less-secure path. Monitoring tunnel health is operational due diligence — derived from availability/resilience expectations (NIST CSF, CIS Control 12). Meraki exposes posture under Security & SD-WAN > Monitor > VPN status.
pros: Confirms encrypted connectivity between sites is actually up
Surfaces latency, reachability, and routing decisions per peer
Catches degraded/down tunnels before users report broken apps
Auto VPN handles key exchange and NAT traversal automatically
cons: Status reflects health, not policy correctness — a healthy tunnel can be over-scoped
Hub/spoke topology and NAT issues can complicate root-cause
VPN-01.ifNotSet: A down or flapping tunnel can break inter-site connectivity or expose traffic to suboptimal paths unnoticed until apps fail; misconfigured scope can over-share subnets.
VPN-01.ifSet: You have continuous visibility into tunnel health and topology; pair it with the VPN connectivity-change alert so a drop notifies you.

refs.title: Meraki Auto VPN — Config and Troubleshooting

ORG-01.origin: This is an operational bus-factor / lockout-resilience control. Meraki's docs explicitly state organizations 'should always have at least two organization admins with full permissions' in case one account is locked out or its email is lost. A single full admin is a single point of failure: lose that person, their credentials, or their mailbox, and the org can become unmanageable, needing slow Support recovery.
pros: Eliminates the single-admin lockout/bus-factor risk Meraki warns against
Provides a backup who can unlock a locked-out admin
Supports continuity through turnover, PTO, and lost-credential events
Aligns with Meraki's documented best practice
cons: Each additional full admin widens the privileged attack surface
Requires governance so the second admin is controlled and monitored
ORG-01.ifNotSet: If the sole full admin is locked out, leaves, or loses email access, no one can administer or recover the org without a slow Meraki Support escalation.
ORG-01.ifSet: You gain recovery resilience, but must secure both privileged accounts (MFA, monitoring) since every full admin has complete org control.

ORG-02.origin: MFA on privileged accounts is the highest-leverage credential control, mandated in spirit by NIST SP 800-63B (AAL2 requires a second factor) and CIS Control 6. It exists because password-only admin accounts are routinely compromised through credential stuffing, password reuse, and phishing — a recurring root cause across years of breaches. A Meraki org admin without 2FA means one phished or reused password yields complete control of the entire organization.
pros: Stops account takeover from phished, reused, or stuffed passwords
Protects the highest-privilege accounts where compromise is most damaging
Authenticator-app/TOTP resists SIM-swap better than SMS
Satisfies NIST 800-63B AAL2 and CIS Control 6
cons: Lost/replaced phone can lock out the admin without backup codes
Adds a small step to every login and requires enrollment
ORG-02.ifNotSet: A single phished or breach-reused password gives an attacker full control of the entire Meraki organization and every network in it — the most common and most damaging compromise path.
ORG-02.ifSet: Account-takeover risk drops sharply, at the cost of managing enrollment and recovery (backup codes, the ORG-01 second admin) so a lost device doesn't lock people out.

ORG-04.origin: Login-security hardening — strong-password policy, idle/session timeout, account lockout — is the defense-in-depth layer behind MFA, codified by NIST SP 800-63B (verifier requirements, throttling failed attempts) and CIS Control 5. Lockout/rate-limiting blunt online password-guessing and stuffing; idle timeout limits the window of a hijacked session; password policy raises guessing cost. Meraki exposes these under Organization > Configure > Settings.
pros: Account lockout/throttling defeats automated online guessing and stuffing
Idle timeout shrinks the exposure window for hijacked sessions
Strong-password/reuse policy raises the cost of compromise
Same page can force 2FA org-wide and restrict admin login IP ranges
cons: Aggressive lockout thresholds can enable denial-of-service lockouts of admins
Short timeouts and frequent expiry can frustrate admins and spur workarounds
ORG-04.ifNotSet: Admin accounts remain exposed to unlimited password-guessing, long-lived idle sessions, and weak passwords — making credential-stuffing and session-hijack far more likely.
ORG-04.ifSet: You raise the bar against guessing and session abuse, but tune thresholds carefully (lockout count, timeout, expiry) and pair with the ORG-01 second admin to recover from lockouts.
